channel.title: David Uhden Collado's Blog
channel.description: Latest articles and updates from David Uhden Collado's blog.

item.title: David Uhden Collado
item.description: The GNU General Public License (GPL) was created to counter the proprietary software model by guaranteeing four essential freedoms: running, studying, sharing, and modifying software. However, the GPL also introduces contradictions and side effects that can undermine the spirit of cooperation and freedom the community seeks to foster.

item.title: David Uhden Collado
item.description: Since its inception, OpenBSD has stood out as a UNIX-like operating system based on 4.4BSD, known for security and minimalist design. Over the years, it has proven to be a strong choice for professional and personal use alike, offering a platform where code and configuration are developed with meticulous attention to detail.

item.title: David Uhden Collado
item.description: The evolution of operating systems has been shaped largely by enterprise demands. For decades, vendors such as Microsoft prioritized backward compatibility, while projects such as OpenBSD and Apple chose to break more aggressively with the past to improve security, performance, and architecture. This article examines how those contrasting approaches shape innovation and system security.

item.title: David Uhden Collado
item.description: The UNIX philosophy has long been presented as a paradigm for building systems from small, specialized tools. Over time, however, its foundations have often been oversimplified and misread. It is frequently reduced to the idea that one only needs to connect small programs through pipes[1], or even equated—incorrectly—with modern concepts such as microservices.
